title: Atelier Noir — Fragrance Film
summary: A 30-second cinematic film for a luxury fragrance launch — entirely AI-generated, finished in-house.
intro: A house known for its restraint asked us to translate a single word — patience — into thirty seconds of moving image. The result is a quiet, almost devotional film: glass, breath, amber light, and the suggestion of a body without ever showing one.
paragraphs: The brief landed in late autumn. The perfumer had spent two years on the formula and wanted the launch film to feel like the scent itself: warm, slow, and a little secretive. We spent the first week not generating a single image — just listening, smelling, reading the brand's reference library, and writing.

From there we built three visual directions and carried one forward: an interior shot in winter light, with a single bottle catching late afternoon sun. Every hero frame was generated and re-generated until the glass behaved like glass — the small inaccuracies that betray AI imagery were chased out one by one in a five-pass refinement loop.

Motion was the final layer. We treated the still frames as plates, animated transitions between them with controlled camera moves, and graded the final piece in DaVinci Resolve to a custom score by an in-house composer. The film shipped on time and ran across paid social, the brand's homepage, and a 90-second cinema cut for selected European markets.

process.title: Concept
process.body: We translated the perfumer's brief into three visual directions, anchored in scent memory and material texture.

process.title: Style frames
process.body: Twelve hero stills were generated, then graded against the brand's reference palette of bone, ash, and amber.

process.title: Motion
process.body: Selected frames were animated into seamless loops and edited to a custom score, then finished in DaVinci Resolve.

process.title: Delivery
process.body: Master file, six paid-social cutdowns, hero stills, and a 90s cinema cut delivered in a single drop.

gallery.alt: Detail of bottle cap and shadow
gallery.caption: Hero still 07 — closure detail

quote.text: It's the first launch film we've made that I want to keep watching. They understood the brand before they touched a single tool.
quote.author: Camille Roux

title: Verdant — Skincare Launch
summary: Botanical product story for a clean-skincare debut — twenty hero visuals across paid, organic, and PR.
intro: Verdant launched in five markets simultaneously and needed a single visual world that would hold up across every channel — from a New York subway billboard to a Pinterest pin saved at 2am.
paragraphs: The brand had a beautiful bottle and almost no imagery. We trained a small custom model on the bottle's geometry, label kerning, and brand color so that every generation produced a consistent product across hundreds of variations.

From there, art direction took over. We built three editorial 'rooms' — a sun-warmed kitchen, a tiled bathroom, a marble vanity — and shot the product in each one with the same lighting language across all twenty hero stills.
The launch performed 38% above the brand's paid-media benchmarks in week one, and the imagery has since been picked up by two retail partners for in-store display.

process.title: Direction
process.body: Editorial direction inspired by Kinfolk and early-2000s minimalism.

process.title: Generation
process.body: Custom-trained model on the brand's bottle geometry and label typography for absolute consistency.

process.title: Finish
process.body: Color-graded in Capture One with subtle film grain to soften the AI signature.

process.title: Roll-out
process.body: Hand-off package included naming conventions, alt-text, and channel-specific crops.

gallery.alt: Skincare bottle in soft window light
gallery.caption: Hero 02 — kitchen light

quote.text: We launched in five markets without flying anywhere. The imagery did the heavy lifting.
quote.author: Ines Park

title: North House — Property Reel
summary: An AI-rendered architectural walkthrough for a development not yet broken ground.
intro: Sometimes the building doesn't exist yet — and the marketing has to begin anyway. North House asked us to render a property they had only as drawings, into something a buyer could feel.
paragraphs: Working from floor plans, material samples, and a moodboard, we produced eight interior frames that respected the architect's intent down to the cabinetry pulls.
We then sequenced those frames into a continuous camera move using AI motion interpolation, finishing with sound design that matched the building's intended quietness.

process.title: Plan to image
process.body: Floor plans and material samples were translated into photoreal interior frames.

process.title: Walkthrough
process.body: Frames were sequenced into a continuous camera move using AI motion interpolation.

process.title: Sound
process.body: Custom ambient sound design matched the architect's intended quietness.

title: Lumen — Eyewear Drop
summary: A 14-day social rollout for a limited eyewear capsule — 36 deliverables, one consistent visual world.
intro: A two-week capsule needed two weeks of imagery — and one visual story tying it all together. We built it around six AI-generated muses and three locations that each carried a different mood.
paragraphs: Casting came first. We sketched six archetypes that mirrored Lumen's customer base, then generated and refined each muse until they felt like real people you might recognize from a film.

Each muse was photographed in three locations — desert, coast, and atelier — for a continuous multi-week story. Posts were sequenced so the rollout felt like an unfolding film, not a series of unrelated tiles.

process.title: Casting
process.body: Six AI-generated muses chosen to mirror the brand's archetypes.

process.title: Series
process.body: Each muse photographed in three locations — desert, coast, and atelier — for a cohesive multi-week story.

process.title: Sequencing
process.body: Posts ordered to read as a film, with paid spots threaded between organic moments.

title: Mara — Menu Imagery
summary: Seasonal menu imagery for a Michelin-listed restaurant — moody, painterly, and impossibly precise.
intro: A chef who treats his menu like a poem doesn't want a food photographer. He wants a painter. We answered with eighteen still lifes in the style of seventeenth-century Dutch masters.
paragraphs: We spent a day in the kitchen with the chef, tasting each course and writing notes on what each dish needed to feel like — not look like. Those notes became the prompts.
Eighteen plates were generated as still lifes, then printed at A2 and used both on the menu and as a small exhibition in the restaurant's entryway.

process.title: Tasting
process.body: Spent a day with the chef to translate flavor notes into visual cues.

process.title: Plating
process.body: Each dish was generated as a still life in the style of 17th century Dutch painters.

process.title: Print
process.body: Final prints color-corrected for the restaurant's warm lighting.

title: Kestrel — Automotive Teaser
summary: A 15-second teaser film for an EV reveal — built without a single physical shoot day.
intro: A reveal teaser usually means a closed track, a six-figure shoot, and a month of post. We did it in three weeks with a team of four, and the only thing that was real was the brief.
paragraphs: We pre-vis'd the reveal sequence with rough generations, locked the shot list, then refined hero shots in five passes for paint accuracy, reflection physics, and lens character.
The teaser broke on the brand's social channels and racked up 4.2M organic views in 72 hours. The full reveal followed two weeks later — also AI-led.

process.title: Pre-vis
process.body: Storyboarded the reveal sequence with rough generations before locking the final shots.

process.title: Render
process.body: Hero shots refined in five passes for paint accuracy, reflection physics, and lens character.

process.title: Roll-out
process.body: Teaser → reveal sequencing planned with paid + earned channels.
